Hi! I'm Mr. Droste, and today we're going to explore how gravity affects different objects when they fall. Are you ready to get started?
Yeah, sounds good, let's do this.

Awesome! Let's jump right in. 

Imagine you have two metal balls that are the exact same size, but one is twice as heavy as the other. If you drop them both from the roof of a building at the exact same time, does the heavier ball hit the ground faster, slower, or at the same time as the lighter one? What do you think, and why?
The heavier one definitely hits first because gravity pulls heavier stuff down way faster.

That is a really common way to think about it! It's true that gravity pulls on the heavier ball with more force, but we also have to think about how much effort it takes to get that heavier ball moving in the first place. 

If you try to push a heavy box versus a light box, which one is harder to get moving?
I'm confused, why are we talking about pushing boxes when we were just talking about dropping stuff?

That's a fair question! Pushing a box and dropping a ball both involve overcoming inertia, which is just an object's natural resistance to changing its motion. 

The heavier ball is harder to get moving than the lighter one, so even though gravity pulls on it harder, does that help explain why they might fall differently than you'd expect?
So they actually hit at the same time because it kinda cancels out?

Spot on! You nailed it. 

The extra gravitational pull on the heavier ball is perfectly balanced out by the fact that it's harder to get moving, so they actually hit the ground at the exact same time. 

To make sure we've got this down, what do you think would happen if we dropped a heavy steel ball and a light wooden ball of the same size?
The steel one hits first because gravity pulls on metal way harder than wood.

Actually, it's the same deal here! Even though gravity pulls harder on the heavier steel ball, that steel ball also has more resistance to moving than the lighter wood ball, so they still hit at the same time. 

Does it make sense how that balancing act works no matter what material the balls are made of?
I'm just really confused now, why does everything perfectly cancel out every single time?

It is mind-blowing, isn't it? It cancels out perfectly because the exact same property of the ball—its mass—determines both how hard gravity pulls on it and how hard it is to move. 

Since mass is responsible for both things, doubling the mass doubles the gravity pull but also doubles the resistance to moving, keeping the speed of the fall exactly the same. How does that connection feel to you?
